---
title: "Preparing for Class"
subtitle: "Week 00"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
---

## Purpose

Week 00 makes sure every student starts with the same working habits: a GitHub repository, a sensible project layout, a terminal they can use, and a first record of their setup in GitHub Issues.

The goal is not speed. The goal is to make your work legible to yourself, your future collaborators, and the instructor.

## By the end of this week

You should be able to:

- Navigate your computer with the shell.
- Create and explain a `code/`, `data/`, `output/`, `docs/`, and `notebooks/` project layout.
- Clone, edit, commit, and push to GitHub.
- Open a GitHub Issue with enough context that someone else can help you.
- Describe which tasks belong on your computer, Raven, and Hyak.

## Core habits

### Work in a repository

Every course artifact should live in your course repository unless there is a clear reason it should not. Raw or very large data are usually recorded but not committed.

### Make directories explain themselves

Each major directory should contain a short `README.md` explaining what belongs there. This prevents the common end-of-quarter problem where `final-final2-new/` becomes the most important directory in the project.

### Commit small, useful checkpoints

A commit should capture a meaningful step: a script added, a result regenerated, an assignment completed, or a figure revised. Commit messages should tell the next reader what changed and why.

## Project setup checklist

Create this structure in your repository:

```bash
mkdir -p code data/raw data/processed output docs notebooks assignment homework
touch code/README.md data/README.md output/README.md docs/README.md
```

Add `data/raw/` to `.gitignore` if it will contain large or restricted files.

## Project checkpoint

Open your setup issue after the three pre-course tutorials are complete. Then make sure your repository has:

- A clear top-level `README.md`.
- The standard project directories.
- At least one pushed commit after setup.
- A note about any setup problems you still have.

## References

- Roberts Lab Handbook: Computing Best Practices
- Roberts Lab Handbook: Data Management
- Happy Git with R
